original: Hibernian FC has appointed former striker Mixu Paatelainen as their new manager after John Collins resigned. Paatelainen, who was a popular player at Hibernian from 1998-2001 and again from 2002-2003, joins TPS Turku in Finland. He is excited to take on the challenge of managing the club and promises to bring energy, hard work, and leadership to the squad. Paatelainen has also expressed his commitment to working with players to improve their skills. The club's chairman, Rod Petrie, is pleased with the appointment, saying that Paatelainen was the strongest candidate and impressed him during an interview.

A bus carrying high school band students from Pelican Rapids High School tipped over on Interstate 94 near Albertville, Minnesota, resulting in one fatality and three critical injuries. The second bus was not affected by the crash, which occurred on Saturday morning. All 48 people on board, including the driver, were taken to nearby hospitals for treatment. Authorities are investigating the cause of the accident, which is believed to be under investigation.

A possible suicide attack was reported at the Benazir Bhutto International Airport in Islamabad, Pakistan, on Thursday after receiving intelligence about a threat. Police tightened security, emptied the parking lot, and cleared the terminal building of large crowds that usually gather there. The attack occurred just days before another deadly blast at the Marriott Hotel in Islamabad, which killed over 50 people and injured many more.

The Danish intelligence service has identified one of its employees, Karsten Krabbe, as a victim of the bombing. Krabbe was a security adviser at the Danish Embassy in Islamabad, which was targeted by a suicide bomber in June.

In response to the attacks, Pakistan's government banned U.S. personnel from major hotels in several cities, including Lahore and Islamabad. The U.S. Embassy in Islamabad is also restricting travel for U.S. citizens, and has suspended visa services for Thursday and Friday.

The advisory advises Americans to avoid crowds and demonstrations, keep a low profile, and vary their routes while traveling to Pakistan. A previous travel warning from the U.S. government still stands due to ongoing security concerns.

The article reports on the prostitution scandal involving New York Gov. Eliot Spitzer and Kristen, also known as Ashley Alexandra Dupre. Spitzer was arrested by federal authorities for arranging a liaison with Dupre through the Emperors Club VIP prostitution ring.

According to court documents, Dupe was just 22 years old when she began working with Spitzer, who is now resigning from his position as governor of New York due to the scandal. Dupe has not been charged with any crime and claims to have left home at age 17 to pursue her music career.

Dupe's background reveals a story of abuse, homelessness, and survival. She has stated that she moved to Manhattan to pursue music and has shared lyrics from one of her songs "What we want" on her MySpace page. Dupe also references singers Etta James, Aretha Franklin, and Celine Dion as influences.

Dupe's sister Kyle Youmans is quoted in the article saying he will not comment on the case or how his sister earns money, but praises her as the best sister he could have. Dupe's mother Carolyn Capalbo has also stated that she was close to her daughter and believes she got involved in something larger than herself.

Dupe made a brief appearance at U.S. Magistrate Court as a witness against four people charged with operating the prostitution ring, but has not been charged directly. The scandal has left Dupe reportedly without sleep since its public disclosure.

Paul McCartney has announced that he will perform a concert in Tel Aviv, Israel on September 25, 43 years after the Beatles' initial proposal for a concert was met with resistance by Israeli authorities due to concerns about Beatlemania potentially harming young people. The show is expected to be one of the biggest concerts ever held in Israel and will feature around 100 production personnel and two plane loads of equipment.

The concert has been billed as "Friendship First" and is part of McCartney's plans to promote cultural understanding between the UK and Israel, given his role in promoting British culture and values during his time with the Beatles. The event is also expected to be a commercial success, with tickets priced at around $426.86-$139.44.

The story has been renewed by recent developments, including an apology from Israel's ambassador to Britain for any perceived "misunderstanding" related to the original proposal.

US President Barack Obama is criticizing his Republican opponent Senator John McCain for not addressing working families and middle-class issues during a recent presidential debate on Friday. Obama said that McCain did not speak about the struggles of middle-class families, despite talking about the economy for 40 minutes. McCain responded by saying he has "bigger things to worry about" than talking about middle-class tax relief.

Obama's campaign points out that McCain did not specifically mention middle class during the debate, while Obama said that McCain didn't understand key issues facing the country. The Obama-Biden campaign released a new ad called "Zero," which accuses McCain of not getting it on economic policy.

A CNN/Opinion Research Corporation poll found that 47% of voters believe Republicans are more responsible for the current economic problems, while 24% say Democrats are more responsible. McCain's spokesman said Obama is unable to debate the merits of supporting higher taxes on middle-class families and bloated government spending during a crisis.

The McCain campaign has criticized Obama's tax policy, saying it would increase taxes in 2009 and offer tax cuts only for certain groups, while arguing that McCain had nothing to say about working families or middle-class issues.

Afghan lawmakers have accused Pakistan's intelligence agency, the Inter-Services Intelligence (ISI), of involvement in a series of deadly attacks in Afghanistan, including a recent attack on the Indian embassy that killed over 40 people. The Afghan Cabinet has made it clear that they will boycott meetings with Pakistan unless "bilateral trust" is restored. Pakistan has denied any involvement in these attacks, but an ISI official said that if there is evidence to support the accusations, they would take action.

The incidents of terrorism and extremism in Afghanistan have been linked to Pakistan's intelligence agency by several sources, including a US think tank report that alleged Pakistani intelligence agents were helping insurgents in Afghanistan. The report, funded by the US Defense Department, claimed that ISI members and personnel trained fighters at camps in Pakistan and financed them to cross into Afghanistan.

Pakistan has rejected the allegations, calling it a "smear campaign" designed to create doubt among others. However, the Afghan Cabinet's resolution emphasizes the seriousness of the accusations and warns of consequences for not restoring bilateral trust between the two countries.

The relationship between Afghanistan and Pakistan is strained due to historical tensions over support for militant groups in both countries. The US has imposed sanctions on several individuals and entities linked to terrorism in Pakistan, which has led to a deterioration in relations with Islamabad.

A Thai cargo ship called MV Thor Star has been hijacked by pirates in the Gulf of Aden off the coast of Somalia. The ship, which had 28 Thai crew members on board, was taken over Tuesday. This is not the first time pirate attacks have occurred in this area, where pirate activity is common due to safety concerns for unescorted vessels. The incident comes after Canada dispatched a warship to protect aid ships in the region earlier this month, highlighting the ongoing security issue.

Three more members of a polygamous sect led by Warren Jeffs are facing sexual assault charges in Texas. The charges include felony counts for sexual assault of a child and bigamy. This brings the total number of charges against Jeffs and his followers to 12, including four children who were removed from the compound by state and federal authorities earlier this year due to allegations of physical and sexual abuse. The charges stem from an investigation into the FLDS (Fundamentalist Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints) compound outside Eldorado, Texas. Jeffs is currently awaiting trial in Utah and could face up to life in prison if convicted on the latest charge. His attorney in Arizona has questioned the motives of Texas authorities, suggesting that they are trying to cover up a botched attack on the ranch in Texas. The case has sparked renewed attention to the FLDS' practices of polygamy and child abuse.
newmod: Mixu Paatelainen has been named as Hibernian's new manager in succession to John Collins. The 40-year-old Finnish striker, who played for Dundee United and Bolton/Wolverhampton in England, was a popular figure at the Scottish club. He joins from TPS Turku in Finland after playing for several clubs in Scotland, including Strasbourg. Paatelainen is excited about the challenge of leading Hibernian's players to play exciting, attacking, and intelligent football. He will work with players individually and as groups to help them improve. The chairman of Hibernian, Rod Petrie, believes that Paatelainen has the skills and qualities needed to lead the team to success.

A bus carrying high school band students crashed on Interstate 94 northwest of Minneapolis, Minnesota, killing one person and critically injuring three others. The bus was returning from a band trip to Chicago after 48 people, including the driver, were already on board. The incident occurred near Albertville, where all passengers were taken to hospitals for treatment or evaluation. The cause of the accident is under investigation, and no further information has been released yet.

The U.N. refugee agency, UNHCR, says Iceland and Sweden are planning to take in about 200 Palestinian refugees from Iraq who have been living in refugee camps along the Iraqi-Syrian border. Over two dozen of these refugees will be headed to Iceland in the next few weeks, while 155 Palestinians in the Al Tanf camp will be accepted for resettlement in Sweden. The UNHCR says around 10,000 to 15,000 Palestinians remain stranded due to violence and lack of access to safe zones. Despite repeated calls for international support, few Palestinians have been accepted for resettlement or offered shelter outside their current locations. The agency notes that Sudan has offered to take in some of these refugees, but a plan is being finalized to resettle them in the United States. The U.S. government has previously expressed concerns about taking Palestinian refugees from Sudan, which itself is experiencing sectarian fighting and human rights abuses.

Police in Pakistan tightened security at airports across the country on Thursday after receiving reports of a possible suicide attack at Islamabad's Benazir Bhutto International Airport. The airport was closed for a few hours, but flights continued to depart. Authorities suspected the airport of being under threat due to intelligence reports, and the city is still on edge following a deadly bombing Saturday night that killed 50 people. One Danish security employee, Karsten Krabbe, also died in the attack. The U.S. government has restricted travel to several cities in Pakistan, including Islamabad, Lahore, and Karachi, citing "general security concerns." Americans in Pakistan are advised to avoid crowds and demonstrations, keep a low profile, and be cautious of crowds. A travel warning from August 7 still remains in effect, advising US citizens to defer nonessential travel to Pakistan due to ongoing security concerns.

The New York Governor, Eliot Spitzer, was caught arranging a liaison with an escort service called Emperor's Club VIP, which is also known as the prostitution ring. The service has been shut down and several people have been charged with operating it. One of them is 22-year-old Kristen, who was described on the Emperor's Club VIP website as "petite, very pretty brunette" and 105 pounds. Spitzer resigned as governor after the scandal emerged, and he later made a brief appearance in court as a witness against several people involved in the prostitution ring. The woman at the center of the scandal is Ashley Alexandra Dupre, who was reportedly a prostitute who met with Spitzer at the Mayflower Hotel in Washington on February 13. She has not been charged with any crime and has been identified in court documents as Kristen. Dupre appears to be a young woman from New Jersey who moved to Manhattan to pursue her music career, and she has shared some of her experiences and thoughts about her life online. Her brother Kyle Youmans has also expressed support for his sister, saying that she is "the best sister you could have."

Paul McCartney has announced plans to perform a concert in Tel Aviv, Israel on September 25 as part of his "Friendship First" tour. The concert had been rumored for months and is expected to be one of the biggest concerts ever held in Israel. Promoters are estimating ticket prices at around $426.86 to $139.44 USD. McCartney will bring around 100 production people, including equipment. He has expressed enthusiasm about performing live in Israel after decades waiting for an opportunity. The tour aims to promote friendship and unity between countries, which was allegedly hindered by a ban on The Beatles concert in the 1960s. Ron Prosor, Israel's ambassador to Britain, visited Liverpool earlier this year and apologized for the "misunderstanding."

Sen. Barack Obama accused Sen. John McCain of being out of touch with the middle class during a campaign event in Detroit, Michigan, on Sunday. Obama said that during Friday night's presidential debate, McCain repeated his statement about "Main Street" paying a penalty for Washington and Wall Street excesses, but didn't specifically say "middle class." However, McCain did repeatedly say that he doesn't understand key issues the country is facing. The Obama campaign pointed out that McCain had nothing to say about working families or middle-class tax relief during the debate, despite voting in favor of a Senate budget resolution in 2008. In response, the McCain campaign released a new ad saying when it comes to the economy, McCain "doesn't get it." A recent CNN/Opinion Research Corporation poll found that 47% of voters believed Republicans are more responsible for the current economic problems, while 24% said Democrats are more responsible. The Obama-Biden campaign is continuing to argue that argument in a new ad on Monday, saying when it comes to the economy, McCain "doesn't get it."

Afghan lawmakers have accused Pakistan's intelligence agency of involvement in a series of deadly attacks in Afghanistan, calling them "the largest center for breeding and exporting terrorism." The recent attack on the Indian embassy killed over 40 people, and several other incidents have occurred in recent weeks, including an assault on a military outpost that killed nine US soldiers. Afghan Cabinet members issued a scathing indictment, saying they would boycott meetings with Pakistan unless "bilateral trust" is restored. In response, a Pakistani official claimed their nation has no reason to blame others for the attacks and urged Afghanistan to provide proof before making any accusations. The issue has deepened tensions between Pakistan and Afghanistan, which have a long-standing history of distrust. A U.S. think tank released a report last month that suggested some members of Pakistan's intelligence agency were helping insurgents in Afghanistan, but Pakistan denies these allegations. Relations between the two countries are strained, with Afghan President Hamid Karzai threatening to send troops across the border to take on militant groups.

Pirates have hijacked a Thai cargo ship called MV Thor Star in the Gulf of Aden off the coast of Somalia. The ship, which was carrying 28 Thai crew members, is owned by Bangkok-based Thoresen Thai Agencies. This is not the first time pirates have targeted ships in this area, as other vessels have reported similar attacks earlier this month. The Kenya Seafarers Association (KSA) represents merchant vessels and says the incident is a major concern for their safety.

Three additional members of a polygamous sect led by Warren Jeffs are facing sexual assault charges in Texas. The three men, who have not been arrested, face felony counts of sexual assault and bigamy. This comes two months after five followers were indicted in Texas on child sexual assault charges, related to allegations of physical and sexual abuse at the compound outside Eldorado. The sect's leader, Jeffs, is awaiting trial in Utah for rape charges stemming from an incident involving a 14-year-old marriage. In Arizona, Jeffs faces similar charges. Authorities say the investigation into the FLDS is motivated by "to cover themselves up on the botched attack on the ranch in Texas." This latest development raises concerns about the treatment of children who were removed from the compound and allegations of abuse still ongoing.
newone: Hibernian have appointed Mixu Paatelainen as their new manager in succession to John Collins, who resigned due to financial constraints. Paatelainen, a former striker for Hibernian, was offered the job after playing for Finnish side TPS Turku and having previously coached the club's youth team. The 40-year-old has agreed to bring energy, hard-work, and leadership to the squad, with a focus on creating exciting and attacking football. Paatelainen believes he can help players improve individually and as a group, and is confident in his ability to enhance Hibernian's reputation. He was joined by chairman Rod Petrie, who is delighted with the appointment and believes Paatelainen is the strongest candidate for the job.

A bus carrying high school band students crashed on Interstate 94 northwest of Minneapolis, Minnesota, Saturday, killing one person and critically injuring three others. All 48 people on board were taken to hospitals for treatment. The incident occurred when the bus tipped over while returning from a band trip in Chicago, Illinois. A second bus traveling with the first one was not affected by the accident. Authorities are investigating the cause of the crash.

The U.N. High Commissioner for Refugees has announced plans to resettle 200 Palestinian refugees from Iraq who have been living in refugee camps along the Iraqi-Syrian border. More than two dozen of these refugees will head to Iceland in the next few weeks, while 155 are being accepted for resettlement in Sweden. The UNHCR says about 2,300 Palestinians "are living in desperate conditions" and unable to return home or cross borders due to violence in Iraq. Some have been accepted for resettlement in third countries such as Brazil and Chile, but the offer is small compared to those stranded in the border area. A group of European countries has also offered to take in some of these Palestinians, although an operations plan is being finalized with the UNHCR. This comes after a request from Refugees International to the US government to resettle the Palestinians instead of Sudan, which is also experiencing sectarian fighting and human rights concerns.

Security at Islamabad's Benazir Bhutto International Airport was increased after receiving reports of a possible suicide attack on the international airport that serves the Pakistani capital. The airport, which sees many relatives arrive or depart, was closed as police cleared the terminal building and emptied the parking lot. There were reports of a bomb threat at another airport in the country, but it has since been resolved. Pakistan has previously experienced a deadly blast at its Marriott Hotel on Saturday night, killing over 50 people. The attack has sparked security concerns and led to closures of hotels in several Pakistani cities, with U.S. government officials also warning Americans to be cautious due to ongoing security concerns.

The story involves Ashley Alexandra Dupre, a 22-year-old woman from New Jersey who is accused of being an escort known as Kristen, the high-priced prostitute who met with New York Gov. Eliot Spitzer at a hotel in Washington. Spitzer was caught arranging the liaison on a federal wiretap and has since resigned as governor of New York. Dupre, who is also referred to as Ashley Youmans, had previously been homeless and broke but claims that she survived through music and her own personal struggles. She posted a brief appearance on her MySpace page where she shows herself in an apartment building where she lives, revealing that she has not been charged with any crime. Dupre says she is mostly asleep since the case became public and wants to be seen as more than just a "monster." Her background and music are described by her brother Kyle Youmans on his MySpace page, who describes her as "tough" and suggests that she will recover from this situation.

Paul McCartney has announced that he will perform a concert in Tel Aviv, Israel on September 25. The concert was rumored for months and is expected to be one of the biggest concerts ever held in Israel. Tickets are priced at around $426.86-$139.44 USD. The event comes after an apology from Israel's ambassador to Britain, Ron Prosor, who acknowledged that it was a "great missed opportunity" to bring The Beatles to Israel when they were first popular. McCartney and Ringo Starr are the only surviving members of The Beatles. He described the concert as "Friendship First," highlighting the importance of experiencing music firsthand rather than just listening to it.

Sen. Barack Obama attempted to attack Sen. John McCain by saying that McCain is out of touch with working families when discussing the economy during Friday night's presidential debate. However, McCain responded by saying he has "bigger things to worry about" than addressing McCain's claims about helping taxpayers and workers. McCain's campaign pointed out that during the debate, Obama didn't specifically say "middle class," but rather discussed issues such as working families and Main Street America. Obama's campaign responded with a new 30-second ad called "Zero," which states that when it comes to the economy, McCain has said nothing about the middle class. The ad also points out that McCain repeated his claim of tax increases in the Senate budget resolution, but didn't offer any alternative solutions. A recent CNN poll found that 47% of voters believe Republicans are more responsible for the current economic problems, while 24% say Democrats are more responsible.

Afghan lawmakers have accused Pakistan's intelligence agency of involvement in a string of deadly attacks in Afghanistan, calling them "the largest center for breeding and exporting terrorism." The recent attack on the Indian embassy killed over 40 people was one of several incidents in which Afghan leaders believe Pakistani intelligence agents are responsible. A senior Pakistani official responded by saying that Afghanistan is making baseless allegations without proof, and that if they want to investigate, they should provide evidence. The Afghan Cabinet has issued a resolution calling for bilateral trust between the two countries to be restored before any meetings can take place. This comes after recent attacks in which at least 40 people were killed, including an assault on a military outpost that killed nine US soldiers and a suicide attack on the Indian embassy. The United States has also expressed no indication that foreign agents are responsible for the embassy attack, and has stated that Pakistan's intelligence agency is not involved. Relations between Afghanistan and Pakistan are strained, with President Hamid Karzai threatening to send troops across the border to fight against militant groups.

A Thai cargo ship called MV Thor Star has been hijacked by pirates off the coast of Somalia. The ship, carrying 28 Thai crew members, was taken over Tuesday. This is not the first time a Thai vessel has been hijacked - earlier this month, Canada announced plans to dispatch a warship to the area to protect pirate-aided shipments of United Nations food aid. Pirate attacks are common in the Gulf of Aden, and incidents have been reported throughout the year.

Three male members of Warren Jeffs' Fundamentalist Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints compound in Texas have been indicted on sexual assault and bigamy charges. The indictment, which was released Tuesday, accuses three men of sexually assaulting children under 18 years old and also makes them two counts of failure to report child abuse. This latest development comes just two months after five followers were indicted on similar charges in July. The charges stem from a state and federal investigation into the sect's alleged abuse of children at the YFZ Ranch. Over 400 children were removed from the compound, which had been accused of physical and sexual abuse. The Texas Attorney General has ordered the men to be released due to lack of evidence, but their attorney in Arizona is questioning the motives of authorities. Jeffs faces multiple charges, including rape and child abandonment, in both Utah and Arizona. He is currently awaiting trial in Utah and could face life in prison if convicted.
newtwo: Hibernian have appointed Mixu Paatelainen as their new manager in succession to John Collins. Paatelainen, a former striker for the club, was a popular player who played for Hibernian from 1998-2001 and again from 2002-2003 before moving to Finland's TPS Turku. He joins Hibernian after Collins resigned due to lack of funds in buying new players. Paatelainen is excited about the challenge, saying he wants to play exciting, attacking football and help improve individual and team performances. The club has agreed a compensation package for his release. Chairman Rod Petrie says Paatelaine impressed during an interview and was the strongest candidate for the job, having a clear vision of what type of football Hibernian should play.

A bus carrying high school band students from Pelican Rapids High School tipped over on Interstate 94 near Albertville, Minnesota, Saturday. The incident occurred when the bus was returning from a trip to Chicago, Illinois. Three people were critically injured, and 48 others were treated for various injuries. All passengers from the westbound bus that crashed were taken to hospitals for treatment. The cause of the accident is being investigated.

The United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR) has announced plans to help transfer 200 Palestinian refugees from Iraq to Sweden and Iceland. Over 2,300 Palestinians are currently living in desperate conditions along the Iraq-Syria border, unable to return to Iraq or cross borders to neighboring countries. The UNHCR says about 1,400 people are living in Al Waleed refugee camp in Iraq, and around 900 in Al Tanf camp near Syria. To address their situation, Sudan has offered to take in some of the Palestinians, with UNHCR and Palestinian representatives finalizing an operations plan. The UNHCR also notes that a few European countries have provided urgent medical assistance to the stranded Palestinians. While some criticize Sudan's offer as not ideal, others suggest resettling the Palestinians in the United States instead, citing dire circumstances. Sweden and Iceland are set to take in around 200 refugees over the next few weeks.

A possible suicide attack at Islamabad's international airport has prompted high security measures across the country. The Benazir Bhutto International Airport on the outskirts of Islamabad was closed as police cleared terminal buildings of large crowds gathering to see relatives depart or arrive. Authorities placed the airport on high alert after receiving intelligence reports indicating it was under threat. Several locations, including the Marriott Hotel in Islamabad where a suicide bombing killed over 50 people last week, are also under security scrutiny. As a result, U.S. personnel in Pakistan are being restricted from using major hotels, and American citizens are advised to be cautious due to ongoing security concerns. This is not the first time Pakistan has faced security threats - a deadly blast at the Marriott Hotel was followed by a fire that left it in ruins and killed over 50 people.

The New York Governor, Eliot Spitzer, was caught arranging a meeting with a prostitute, identified as Kristen or Ashley Alexandra Dupre, on a federal wiretap. Dupre, 22, has been identified as the woman behind the high-priced escort service Emperors Club VIP. She had previously left home at 17 and described her life as "my odyssey to New York." Dupre, who is also known as Ashley Youmans, was a singer from New Jersey who had become homeless and broke before pursuing music career. She has not been charged with any crime and made a brief appearance in court as a witness against four people accused of running the prostitution ring. Spitzer's resignation as governor came two days after reports of his connection to the scandal emerged. Dupre is currently living with her family and is trying to cope with the public attention surrounding her case. Her mother described her as being "in shock" and said she had gotten involved in something much larger than herself.

Paul McCartney has announced that he will perform a concert in Tel Aviv, Israel in September, 43 years after some believed a Beatles concert was banned by Israel. The concert is expected to be one of the biggest in Israel's history, with tickets starting at 1,500 shekels (around $426 USD). Promoters say it could encourage other top stars to visit Israel. A previous ban on the Beatles concert was blamed on a dispute between competing concert promoters, but McCartney and Ringo Starr have forgiven Israel for missing out on their live performance. The show is billed as "Friendship First," with McCartney saying he's looking forward to experiencing music and history in Israel. Tickets are expected to cost around $10 million to produce, making a profit possible. The concert is part of an effort to promote musical tourism in Israel.

Senator Barack Obama attempted to accuse Senator John McCain of being out of touch with the middle class during an event in Detroit, Michigan. Obama said that during a debate on Friday night, McCain did not address the struggles of working families, despite talking about the economy for 40 minutes. However, McCain responded by saying he has "bigger things to worry about" and that Obama's record on issues like middle class tax relief and competitive tax rates is concerning. Obama's campaign pointed out that during a debate, McCain did not specifically say "middle class," but instead focused on key issues the country is facing. Obama also released a new 30-second ad on Monday that says when it comes to the economy, McCain doesn't get it. The ad cites parts of Friday night's debate where Obama suggested McCain has followed President Bush on economic policy. In response to the ad, McCain campaign spokesman Tucker Bounds criticized Obama for his tax policy, saying he repeatedly voted in favor of higher taxes and bloated government spending that hurts middle class families.

Afghan lawmakers have directly accused Pakistan's intelligence agency of involvement in a series of deadly attacks in Afghanistan, calling them "the largest center for breeding and exporting terrorism." The accusations come after recent attacks that killed over 40 people at the Indian embassy and a week-old suicide attack on the embassy. A senior Pakistani official responded by saying that Afghan lawmakers are making baseless allegations without proof, and that Pakistan is not responsible for these attacks. However, an ISI spokesperson said that if Afghanistan has evidence of Pakistani intelligence agency involvement, they should present it to the ISI and let them act on it. The situation is further complicated by reports that some members of Pakistan's intelligence service and paramilitary corps have been helping insurgency groups in Afghanistan. Afghanistan has made similar accusations against Pakistan before, and recent attacks suggest a growing concern about Pakistani involvement in Afghanistan. The U.S. Defense Secretary has also stated that there is no indication that foreign agents are responsible for the embassy attack. The situation remains tense with potential consequences for relations between Pakistan and Afghanistan, as well as for the security of both countries.

Pirates have hijacked a Thai cargo ship, the MV Thor Star, off the coast of Somalia. The ship, which had 28 Thai crew members on board, was hijacked Tuesday according to the Kenya Seafarers Association. This is the second pirate attack in the Gulf of Aden this month, after Canada dispatched a warship to protect unescorted U.N. aid ships. Pirates are frequent in these waters, making it difficult for undiscerning vessels to sail safely.

Three male members of Warren Jeffs' Fundamentalist Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints compound in Texas have been indicted on sexual assault and bigamy charges. The charges stem from a state and federal investigation into abuse at the compound, which was recently emptied of over 400 children due to allegations of physical and sexual abuse. Each of the three men faces one felony count of sexual assault of a child and one felony bigamy charge. This is the second time that Jeffs and his followers have been indicted in Texas; they were previously indicted in July on similar charges related to child abuse. The investigation is being led by Texas Attorney General Greg Abbott, who has said it's an effort to "cover themselves up." Jeffs is currently awaiting trial in Utah, where he faces a sentence of up to life in prison for marrying a 14-year-old underage girl, and could face similar charges in Arizona as well.
